narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and academic history to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 34 (under the usual 36.7), Year 1 school decile of 5 (close to average), and Year 3 decile of 1 (well below the 5.53 average)—and predicted a 66.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this failure prediction was your low Year 3 decile ranking, which signals weaker performance relative to peers in your final undergraduate year and strongly raised concerns about your bar readiness. Your Year 1 decile, being solidly in the middle of the pack, helped somewhat by nudging the prediction toward success, showing early potential that offset some worries. Similarly, your LSAT score provided a moderate boost toward passing, as it demonstrated solid analytical skills even if not at the top tier. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your Year 3 grades stand out as a major red flag, while your other metrics align more closely with average applicants but weren't strong enough to counterbalance it. Full-time enrollment plans, common among 93% of students, didn't sway the decision much here. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential as a lawyer—many factors like determination and study habits play into bar success beyond these predictors. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your final-year transcript through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your growth since Year 3. Schools value resilience, so gathering strong recommendation letters emphasizing your upper-middle background's role in driving your ambitions could also help. With targeted improvements, you can shift the model's view and open doors to admission.
